questions.question: When an LLM answers a prompt by generating one token at a time, what is the model doing at each step?

choices.text: It scores possible next tokens from the current context, selects one, appends it, and repeats.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The model is using fixed learned weights and temporary context to make the next-token step.

choices.text: It reads with conscious understanding, then writes the whole answer at once.
choices.feedback: Not quite. That gives the model too much mind. Fluent behavior can happen without awareness.
choices.representedMisconception: conscious mind vs fluent model behavior
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because fluent text can sound like a human explanation.

choices.text: It searches a hidden database and copies the best matching passage.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Search can be added around a model, but a plain LLM generates from context and learned weights.
choices.representedMisconception: search engine/database vs generative model
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because broad model knowledge can feel like search.

choices.text: It changes its weights each time the user sends a new prompt.
choices.feedback: That would be true only if a separate training process updated weights. Ordinary inference usually leaves weights fixed.
choices.representedMisconception: training vs inference
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because the model may seem to adapt within a conversation.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner traces the next-token loop rather than recalling the card definition.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Supports the core model-literacy distinction among context, response tokens, and fixed weights.

questions.question: An LLM gives a fluent explanation of a poem. What should a model-literate learner conclude?

choices.text: It generated response tokens from context and learned patterns; fluency does not prove awareness.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. This is the fluent-behavior-without-awareness boundary Prompt Life wants learners to hold.

choices.text: It must have inner awareness because the explanation sounds human.
choices.feedback: Not quite. That gives the model too much mind. Fluent behavior can happen without awareness.
choices.representedMisconception: conscious mind vs fluent model behavior
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because human-like language invites human-like interpretation.

choices.text: It must have found a hidden perfect source and copied the interpretation.
choices.feedback: Close, but this describes search or retrieval better than a plain LLM response. A fluent answer is not the same as cited evidence.
choices.representedMisconception: database lookup vs learned generation
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because polished answers can feel copied from a source.

choices.text: The poem prompt permanently taught the base model a new skill.
choices.feedback: That would require training. A normal prompt can shape temporary context without durably updating weights.
choices.representedMisconception: prompt/context change vs durable weight update
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because earlier context can shape later replies in a chat.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner diagnoses a real interpretive trap: fluent explanation without inner awareness.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Demystifies model fluency without implying consciousness.

questions.question: A campus tool summarizes text with an LLM, while another creates images by denoising a noisy pattern. What does that show?

choices.text: Generative AI is a family; LLMs and diffusion models are different branches.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Generative AI is a family, and different branches can use different mechanisms.

choices.text: All generative AI tools are LLMs because ChatGPT is generative AI.
choices.feedback: Not quite. An LLM is one kind of generative AI, not the whole category.
choices.representedMisconception: LLM vs generative AI family
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because ChatGPT is many learners’ first generative AI example.

choices.text: The image tool is not AI unless it generates language tokens.
choices.feedback: Close, but image models can be AI without using the LLM text-token loop.
choices.representedMisconception: LLM output vs broader generative outputs
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because this app starts with language models.

choices.text: AI and machine learning are the same label, so the branches do not matter.
choices.feedback: This choice collapses useful categories. Machine learning is a major branch inside the broader AI field.
choices.representedMisconception: AI vs machine learning category boundary
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because public language often blurs these words.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner compares two generative model families by mechanism and output type.

choices.text: Machine learning and AI are exact synonyms.

choices.text: Diffusion models and LLMs use the same generation loop.

questions.question: A chatbot product includes an LLM, a search tool, safety filters, and a user interface. What is the LLM?

choices.text: The language-generating model inside the larger product.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. A product may wrap an LLM with tools, policies, and interface pieces.

choices.text: The whole app, including every tool and button.
choices.feedback: Not quite. The product can include an LLM, but the LLM is not every surrounding tool and screen.
choices.representedMisconception: model vs product/app wrapper
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because users experience the whole product as one AI thing.

choices.text: The safety policy that blocks some outputs.
choices.feedback: Close, but a safety policy shapes behavior around the model; it is not the language model itself.
choices.representedMisconception: policy/guardrail vs model
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because safety behavior is visible in the product.

choices.text: The database or search index the app may call.
choices.feedback: This describes a retrieval or search component. The LLM generates language from context and learned weights.
choices.representedMisconception: retrieval/search component vs language model
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because some chatbot products include search.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner separates the model from the product stack around it.

questions.question: If a system solves a task only by following hand-written if-then rules, how is it different from an LLM?

choices.text: The rule-based system applies explicit rules; an LLM’s fluency mostly comes from learned weights shaped by examples.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. LLMs mostly belong to the learned-patterns tradition, even when products around them use rules.

choices.text: The rule-based system measures loss and updates weights after every task.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Loss and weight updates belong to training learned models, not to a pure if-then rule system.
choices.representedMisconception: symbolic rules vs training loop
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because loss is nearby in the deep-learning explanation.

choices.text: The LLM has conscious reasoning, while the rule-based system has none.
choices.feedback: That gives the LLM too much mind. The key boundary here is learned weights versus explicit rules.
choices.representedMisconception: conscious reasoning vs learned behavior
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because LLM language can sound like reasoning from the inside.

choices.text: There is no difference; both systems are just written rulebooks.
choices.feedback: Close, but LLM behavior is not mainly written as explicit if-then rules; it is shaped by training.
choices.representedMisconception: LLM as hand-coded rulebook
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both systems can produce predictable outputs.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner compares two system designs rather than reciting intellectual history.

questions.question: During deep-learning training, why does the system measure loss?

choices.text: Loss measures prediction error so an optimizer can adjust weights.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. Loss is the numerical training signal, not a feeling or truth meter.

choices.text: Loss tells the model whether a generated answer is morally good.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Loss is a numerical error signal; alignment and policy questions need additional methods.
choices.representedMisconception: loss vs human value judgment
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because loss sounds like a judgment word.

choices.text: Loss is the model’s confidence score for the next token during generation.
choices.feedback: Close, but confidence or probability shapes generation. Loss measures training error so weights can be adjusted.
choices.representedMisconception: loss vs confidence/probability during generation
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both are numerical signals related to prediction.

choices.text: Loss writes the final answer after the model has finished thinking.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Loss is used during training; it is not the model writing a response.
choices.representedMisconception: training signal vs response generation
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because learners may treat every internal step as answer writing.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner identifies the causal role of loss inside training.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Loss is needed for badge-worthy understanding of how weights change.

questions.question: During training, the model predicts a target token and gets it wrong. What makes that moment learning rather than ordinary inference?

choices.text: The training process uses the loss signal to update weights.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. The durable weight update is what makes this training.

choices.text: The model appends the mistaken token to a normal live response.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Appending a response token is inference behavior; training uses loss to change weights.
choices.representedMisconception: training vs autoregressive inference
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because generation also chooses and appends tokens.

choices.text: The prompt becomes durable memory stored inside the model’s weights.
choices.feedback: This choice reveals a common mix-up. Context can shape the current chat without becoming a weight update.
choices.representedMisconception: context vs durable weight update
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because chat context can feel remembered.

choices.text: The model treats the most likely answer as automatically true.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Likelihood is not truth, and this training moment matters because loss can drive a weight update.
choices.representedMisconception: likelihood vs truth
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because likely answers can sound authoritative.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner locates the precise causal step that changes the model.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Core badge distinction: training changes weights; inference uses them.

questions.question: Why can training change how the model behaves tomorrow, while inference usually cannot?

choices.text: Training changes parameters that future runs reuse; inference uses temporary computations for the current run.
choices.feedback: Good distinction. Durable parameters carry forward; inference-time states usually do not.

choices.text: Inference is where the optimizer changes weights for future runs.
choices.feedback: Not quite. Optimizer-driven weight updates belong to training, not ordinary inference.
choices.representedMisconception: optimizer/weight update vs inference
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because both training and inference run model computations.

choices.text: Training only changes the visible prompt text for one response.
choices.feedback: Close, but prompt changes are temporary input changes; training changes learned numbers.
choices.representedMisconception: prompt/context vs weights
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because prompt text clearly changes a response.

choices.text: Training guarantees that every future answer will be true.
choices.feedback: This choice overclaims. Training can improve behavior, but it does not guarantee truth.
choices.representedMisconception: training improvement vs truth guarantee
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because training sounds like making the model better.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner connects durability to parameters reused by future inference.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Strengthens durable-versus-temporary literacy.

questions.question: In a training loop, which sequence is the durable learning path?

choices.text: Example -> prediction -> loss -> weight update.
choices.feedback: Insight strengthened. That is the weight-changing path.

choices.text: Prompt -> response token -> append -> next response token.
choices.feedback: Close, but that sequence describes inference/generation, not training.
choices.representedMisconception: training loop vs autoregressive generation
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because this is the model’s generation loop.

choices.text: Search -> outside passage -> current context -> answer.
choices.feedback: That describes a search or retrieval step. It can add temporary context; it does not by itself update weights.
choices.representedMisconception: training vs retrieval/context
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because outside text can improve an answer.

choices.text: User correction -> better answer -> permanent learning inside the same chat.
choices.feedback: Not quite. A correction can steer the current conversation, but durable learning requires a training process.
choices.representedMisconception: conversation steering vs durable training
choices.distractorRationale: Tempting because a correction can improve the current conversation.

authoringNotes.whyItHelpsModelThinking: The learner chooses the path with the durable update, not another plausible AI sequence.
authoringNotes.badgeMasterySupport: Prepares learners to distinguish training, context, and generation later.
